introduction: Self-Determination Theory (SDT; Deci & Ryan, 1985) has been widely applied to understand athlete motivation. SDT posits that satisfaction of three basic psychological needs—autonomy, competence, and relatedness—predicts the quality of motivation and, ultimately, well-being and performance outcomes. The present study applies an SDT framework to examine coach-athlete relationships in elite swimming, hypothesising that autonomy-supportive coaching behaviours will predict higher levels of intrinsic motivation and lower burnout.

journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
introduction: Fatigue during high-intensity exercise has been attributed to both central and peripheral mechanisms. The Central Governor Model (Noakes, 2000) proposes that exercise intensity is regulated by the brain to prevent catastrophic physiological failure, challenging earlier peripheral fatigue models. We tested whether manipulating perceived effort via a deceptive protocol would alter pacing behaviour consistent with the Central Governor Model's predictions.

journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
introduction: Resistance training frequency is a key variable in programme design. Progressive overload—the systematic increase in training stimulus over time—is widely accepted as a prerequisite for strength adaptation. We examined whether daily undulating periodization produced greater strength gains than linear periodization over 12 weeks, reasoning that more frequent variation in loading parameters may optimise the stimulus-adaptation cycle.

Participants completed a maximal sprint test and a submaximal aerobic capacity assessment. Body composition was measured via dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry. Blood lactate was sampled at rest and immediately post-exercise. No theoretical basis was stated for the relationships examined.

Achievement Goal Theory (Elliot & McGregor, 2001) distinguishes approach and avoidance motivational orientations directed at either mastery or performance goals. Research within sport has demonstrated that mastery-approach goals are associated with adaptive cognitive and affective outcomes, whereas performance-avoidance goals predict anxiety and reduced persistence. The present investigation extends this framework to e-sport competitors, hypothesising that mastery-approach orientations will predict flow states and performance consistency.
